title: Provide a mechanism to bypass repeated content blocks
summary: AI-generated interfaces lack a mechanism to skip repeated navigation blocks, forcing keyboard users to tab through every item before reaching main content.
instruction: Provide a mechanism for keyboard users to bypass repeated navigation and header content. A skip-to-content link is the preferred technique: place it as the first focusable element and make it visible on keyboard focus. Other conforming mechanisms include well-structured ARIA landmark regions and proper heading hierarchy. Absence of a skip link alone does not prove a WCAG failure if another bypass mechanism is present.
rationale: WCAG 2.4.1 (Bypass Blocks) requires a mechanism to skip repeated content blocks. A skip link is the most widely supported technique, providing a consistent experience across browsers, screen readers, and keyboard navigation modes. ARIA landmarks and proper heading structure can also satisfy the requirement but may not be available in all user agents.

violations: A marketing page with 8 navigation links, a search bar, and utility links before the main content, with no skip link and no ARIA landmark structure bypassing the navigation region.
A dashboard with a 15-item sidebar and no skip-link or other bypass mechanism at the top of the page.
controls: A page with Skip to content as the first focusable element, visible on keyboard focus.
A page using ARIA landmark regions (role='navigation', role='main') with proper heading structure that enables screen reader landmark navigation.
borderline: Single-page applications where content regions load dynamically - the skip link should target the content container that updates.

exceptions.description: Pages with no repeated navigation blocks (single-content landing pages) and screen-reader-only applications where navigation order is managed by ARIA landmarks.
examples: A single-section landing page with no navigation
An ARIA-managed SPA with proper landmark navigation

detector.signalDescription: Check if any bypass mechanism exists: a skip-link (anchor with href targeting main-content), role='main' landmark, or programmatic focus management. Skip-link presence is the primary mechanical signal but absence is not necessarily a WCAG failure.
limitations: Skip links added by JavaScript after page load may not appear in static DOM analysis.
Some frameworks manage focus programmatically, making a skip link unnecessary. The detector can verify skip-link presence but cannot prove WCAG failure from absence alone.

scenarios: Pages with no navigation (single-content landing pages where there's nothing to skip).
SPA frameworks that programmatically manage focus on route change.
mitigations: Check for navigation blocks before flagging. Accept framework-based focus management as an alternative approach.

fixturePlan.description: A page with 10 navigation links followed by main content, no skip link present.
Same page with a visible-on-focus skip-to-content link as the first element after body.
An SPA with no skip link but programmatic focus management on route navigation.

notes: Normalization correction: reframed from skip-link mandate to bypass-blocks requirement per WCAG 2.4.1. Skip link described as preferred technique, not sole conforming mechanism. Detector limitations clarified to avoid implying WCAG failure from skip-link absence alone. Maintainer review.
